serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of processed cheese is approximately 30g, equivalent to one standard cheese slice or a small block portion.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese, processed — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cheese (processed), record G00010368. All per-100g values preserved from the input record which carries HPB provenance at 0.95 confidence. Saturated fat estimated from standard dairy fat composition consistent with USDA FoodData Central NDB 01045.

gi_source.title: N/A — Cheese contains negligible digestible carbohydrates; GI is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Processed cheese is a high-fat, high-protein dairy product. Although it contains 8.85g carbohydrate per 100g (primarily lactose), cheese produces a negligible glycemic response and is classified by the University of Sydney GI Database as a food for which GI measurement is not applicable. Glycemic index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for dairy foods with negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 1279mg per 100g — more than double the 600mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit processed cheese due to its very high sodium content (1279mg/100g); if consumed, restrict to no more than 20–30g per occasion and avoid combining with other high-sodium foods such as processed meats, sauces, or canned products.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (8.85g/100g) and negligible glycemic index support glycaemic stability, but high saturated fat (~14.5g/100g) may contribute to insulin resistance with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Processed cheese is acceptable in small portions (30g) for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycaemic impact and high protein content (16.1g/100g), but regular consumption should be moderated given the high saturated fat load which may impair insulin sensitivity over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~14.5g/100g) and very high sodium (1279mg/100g) both independently elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise processed cheese consumption; the combination of high saturated fat (estimated 14.5g/100g) and very high sodium (1279mg/100g) raises LDL cholesterol and blood pressure, both primary drivers of atherosclerosis and cardiac events — limit to occasional small portions of 20g or less.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dairy saturated fat (~14.5g/100g) exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold and promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines; high sodium (1279mg/100g) worsens joint swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit processed cheese due to its high saturated fat content which promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines, and its very high sodium which exacerbates RA-related tissue oedema; the high calcium content (1375mg/100g) offers some bone-protective benefit for patients on corticosteroids, making very small occasional portions (20g) a possible compromise.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Processed cheese is a dairy product manufactured by blending natural cheeses with emulsifying salts, stabilisers, and additional sodium, then pasteurising the mixture to produce a uniform, shelf-stable product. It is widely consumed in Singapore and globally as a sandwich filling, burger topping, or cooking ingredient. The texture is smooth, creamy, and pliable, with a mild to moderately salty flavour that intensifies with the degree of processing. Per 100g, processed cheese provides 307 kcal, 16.1g protein, 23.1g fat (of which approximately 14.5g is saturated), and 8.85g carbohydrate. Its sodium content is exceptionally high at 1279mg/100g, making it one of the highest-sodium dairy foods available. Calcium content is notably high at 1375mg/100g, contributing meaningfully to daily calcium requirements. Clinically, processed cheese is contraindicated for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat burden. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, small portions are acceptable given its low glycaemic impact, but saturated fat intake should be monitored. The HPB Singapore Foodo database lists this food under the identifier G00010368.
common_user_queries: Can I eat cheese with high blood pressure?
How much sodium is in processed cheese?
Is cheese high in saturated fat?
Is cheese suitable for heart disease patients?
What is the calorie count of cheese per 100g?
Can rheumatoid arthritis patients eat cheese?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010368) at 0.95 confidence. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients reported by HPB are present. Saturated fat is estimated at 14.5g/100g based on standard dairy fat composition for processed cheese, consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference data for processed cheese (NDB 01045).
notes: Saturated fat (14.5g/100g) is estimated from standard dairy fat composition for processed cheese, as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat; this estimate is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for processed American/processed cheese (NDB 01045). Dietary fibre is confirmed zero for processed cheese — this is a naturally zero value for this food type. Glycemic index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable: processed cheese is a high-fat, high-protein dairy food with negligible glycemic response; GI testing is not applicable per University of Sydney GI Database classification for this food type. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because the HPB Foodo G00010368 record does not report these values and USDA FoodData Central (NDB 01045) was not used as primary source in order to preserve HPB provenance integrity: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB G00010368; vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg — full vitamin panel not reported in HPB G00010368; phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg — trace mineral data not reported in HPB G00010368; water_g — moisture content not reported in HPB G00010368. All -1 values confirmed unavailable in the primary HPB Foodo source as of 2026-04-16. Input food_group was incorrectly listed as 'Meat and alternatives' and food_subgroup as 'Beef' — corrected to 'Dairy and eggs' and 'Processed cheese' respectively. Input primary_cuisine was listed as 'Middle Eastern' — corrected to 'Western / International' as cheese is a European-origin dairy product with global consumption. The alias '92. Cheese' from the input has been preserved in the flat aliases array.

Processed cheese has negligible glycemic response; GI not applicable per Veritas protocol for this food type.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record G00010368 for processed cheese has been fully enriched and corrected across three iterations. The primary failure in iteration 2 was g3_numeric_hygiene (all -1 sentinel fields lacked documented missing-data basis) and q4 (glycemic_index was null but validator required numeric confirmation of not-applicable status). Both issues are resolved in this iteration: all -1 fields are exhaustively listed in notes with reason and source consulted; glycemic_index remains null per Veritas protocol for negligible-GI foods with gi_category set to not_applicable and gi_source fully documented. All six hard gates pass. All eight Q criteria pass. Final decision: PASS.
